1: שְׁמַע-אֱלֹהִים קוֹלִי בְשִׂיחִי (shĕmaʿ-ʾĕlōhîm qôlî bĕśîḥî, "Hear my voice, O God, in my complaint") — śîaḥ: the meditative complaint, the troubled musing that has become vocal (as in Ps 55:2 — the same word for the unsettled inward meditation voiced as complaint). מִפַּחַד אוֹיֵב תִּצֹּר חַיָּי (mippaḥad ʾôyêb tiṣṣōr ḥayyāy, "guard my life from the terror of the enemy") — paḥad: dread, terror (the freezing fear); nāṣar: to guard, to watch over, to keep (the sentinel's task applied to the Psalmist's life).
2: תַּסְתִּירֵנִי מִסּוֹד מְרֵעִים (tastirênî missôd mĕrêʿîm, "shelter me from the conspiracy of the wicked") — sâtar (hiphil: to hide, to shelter); sôd: the secret counsel, the intimate assembly (as in Ps 55:14 — the sweet counsel shared with the companion; here the same word for a malicious conspiracy). מֵרִגְשַׁת פֹּעֲלֵי אָוֶן (mêrigshat pōʿălê ʾāwen, "from the scheming mob of evildoers") — rigshah: a tumultuous crowd, a riotous gathering (the noisy assemblage of the evildoers). Pōʿălê ʾāwen: "workers of trouble" (the standard formula from Pss 5:5; 6:8 — those whose craft is harm).
3: אֲשֶׁר שָׁנְנוּ כַחֶרֶב לְשׁוֹנָם (ʾăsher shānĕnû kaḥereb lĕshônām, "they sharpen their tongues like swords") — shānan: to whet, to sharpen (to put an edge on a blade — Ps 7:12: God whets his sword; Deut 32:41: the divine sharp sword). The tongue as the blade being deliberately honed for maximum cutting. דָּרְכוּ חִצָּם דָּבָר מָר (dārĕkû ḥiṣṣām dābār mār, "and aim bitter words like arrows from a bow") — dārak: to tread, to draw (a bow — as in Ps 7:12). Dābār mār: "bitter words" — the arrows are poisoned with bitterness (mar: bitter, as the Exodus waters of Marah, Exod 15:23).
4: לִירוֹת בַּמִּסְתָּרִים תָּם (lîrôt bammistārîm tām, "shooting from hiding places at the innocent") — yārāh: to shoot (the arrow-shooting verb); mistārîm: hiding places, ambush positions; tām: the innocent/blameless one (from tāmam: to be complete, whole — the same root as tāmîm). פִּתְאֹם יֹרֻהוּ וְלֹא יִירָאוּ (pitʾōm yōruhû wĕlōʾ yîrāʾû, "they strike suddenly and without fear") — pitʾōm: suddenly, without warning. The ambush of the righteous by the wicked who fear nothing — the combination of cunning strategy and moral recklessness.
5: יְחַזְּקוּ-לָמוֹ דָּבָר רָע (yĕḥazzĕqû-lāmô dābār rāʿ, "they scheme their evil plan together") — ḥāzaq (piel: to strengthen, to reinforce — they strengthen the evil word among themselves): the mutual hardening of resolve in wickedness. יְסַפְּרוּ לִטְמוֹן מוֹקְשִׁים (yĕsappĕrû liṭmôn môqĕshîm, "they talk of setting traps in secret") — sāpar: to count, to plan (as in Ps 22:17 — they "tell" their plans); ṭāman: to hide, to bury (burying the trap under the surface); môqêsh: a snare, a trap (the same word as the "snare" of Ps 69:22). אָמְרוּ מִי יִרְאֶה-לָּמוֹ (ʾāmĕrû mî yirʾeh-llāmô, "they say, 'Who will see us?'") — the assumption of divine inattention: the conspiracy conducted under the delusion that God is absent or blind.
6: יַחְפְּשׂוּ-עוֹלֹת תַּמְנוּ חֵפֶשׂ מְחֻפָּשׂ (yaḥpĕśû-ʿawlōt tamnû ḥêpeś mĕḥuppāsh, "They plan iniquities: 'We have devised a perfect scheme!'") — ḥāpaś: to search, to devise carefully (to inspect for thoroughness — they have searched out every angle of their plan). Ḥêpeś mĕḥuppāsh: "a searched/exhaustive plan" — the enemies believe their conspiracy to be airtight, undetectable. וְקֶרֶב אִישׁ וְלֵב עָמֹק (wĕqereb ʾîsh wĕlêb ʿāmōq, "but the inward thought and heart of a person are unfathomable") — qereb: the inward part; lêb ʿāmōq: a deep/unfathomable heart. The human heart is deeper than any scheme — even the schemers cannot fully know themselves, which means their "perfect plan" is built on a foundation of their own unfathomable depths. God alone can search the deep heart (Jer 17:9-10).
7: וַיֹּרֵם אֱלֹהִים חֵץ פִּתְאוֹם (wayyōrêm ʾĕlōhîm ḥêṣ pitʾôm, "But God will shoot his arrow at them; suddenly they will be struck down") — the double ironic reversal: the enemies shot arrows at the blameless (v4); now God shoots his arrow at them. The verb yārāh (to shoot — v4: the enemies shooting; v7: God shooting) binds the reversal together. Pitʾôm: suddenly (as the enemies struck suddenly in v4; now they are struck suddenly). The divine counter-ambush.
8: וַיַּכְשִׁילוּהוּ עָלֵימוֹ לְשׁוֹנָם (wayyaksîlûhû ʿălêmô lĕshônām, "God makes their own tongues their undoing") — kāshal (hiphil: to make stumble, to cause to fail — God causing them to stumble): God makes their own weapon (the tongue, v3) trip them into ruin. The irony is perfect: the weapon that attacked the blameless (the sharpened tongue-sword of v3) becomes the instrument of the conspirators' own destruction. יִתְנוֹדֲדוּ כָּל-רֹאֵה בָם (yitnôdădû kol-rōʾeh bām, "all who see them shake their heads and flee") — nûd: to wag, to shake the head (the gesture of astonishment/contempt — as in Ps 44:14; Lam 2:15); bāraḥ: to flee. The onlookers recoil from the sight of the divine judgment.
9: וַיִּירְאוּ כָּל-אָדָם (wayyîrĕʾû kol-ʾādām, "Then all people will stand in fear") — the universal reversal: from the conspirators' boast "who will see us?" (v5) to "all people" standing in fear. Yārēʾ: to fear (the holy fear that precedes worship). וַיַּגִּידוּ פֹּעַל אֱלֹהִים (wayyaggîdû poʿal ʾĕlōhîm, "they will proclaim what God has done") — nāgad (hiphil: to declare, to proclaim): the reversal produces proclamation — what the wicked kept secret (their plans) now becomes the public declaration of God's work. וּמַעֲשֵׂהוּ הִשְׂכִּילוּ (ûmaʿăśêhû hiśkîlû, "and ponder his deeds") — śākal (hiphil: to understand, to ponder wisely): not just announcement but theological comprehension — the community understands what God's counter-arrow means.
10: יִשְׂמַח צַדִּיק בַּיהוָה (yiśmaḥ ṣaddîq baYHWH, "The righteous will rejoice in the LORD") — the righteous person's joy is not in vindication or revenge but in the LORD himself. וְחָסָה בוֹ (wĕḥāsāh bô, "and take refuge in him") — ḥāsāh: to flee for refuge (the fundamental trust-verb of the Psalter — as in Ps 2:12; 5:11). וְיִתְהַלְלוּ כָּל-יִשְׁרֵי-לֵב (wĕyithalĕlû kol-yishrê-lêb, "all the upright in heart shall boast") — yāshar lêb: "upright in heart" — the straight/level-hearted, whose inner orientation matches the divine standard of righteousness. Their boast (hālal) is not self-boasting but the boasting in God of those who have nowhere else to boast.
